assertiveness_why: Harry asks open, prepared questions in sequence with light follow-ups but never challenges or contests any of Stefan's claims.
question_style: Mostly open-ended and list-driven (background, stage focus, check size, red flags, quickfire), with occasional clarifying follow-ups (e.g. equity percentage, meeting numbers) but no pushback or planted counter-thesis.
tone: Warm, deferential and enthusiastic ('an absolute pleasure to have you on the show'), fairly formal structure but friendly delivery, brief expressions of surprise ('Wow, 700').
depth: Mostly surface-level operational questions (check size, equity taken, meeting cadence, deal flow volume) with a bit of sector depth on fintech/p2p lending, but no technical or strategic pressure-testing.
harry_quotes: What's your preferred stage to enter into an investment?
Have you ever passed on a company which later you've regretted?
The hardest decision you've had to make in your career.
learnings: Passion Capital deliberately focuses only on seed stage, usually being the first institutional check after friends-and-family money.
A co-working space that portfolio founders use lets the fund monitor progress informally instead of relying on frequent formal board meetings.
Referral-based introductions dominate which pitches actually get a meeting, despite thousands of inbound applications per year.
A crowded competitive-landscape slide where the founder's own company sits conveniently in the top-right corner is treated as a red flag.
Financial services is attractive to investors because incumbents are large, slow-moving and have treated customers poorly, creating room for disruption.
notable: Episode opens with an iTunes review giveaway promotion, reflecting the show's very early grassroots growth tactics.
